title: Orbit

infobox.value: is the curved [trajectory](/wiki/Trajectory "Trajectory") of an [object](/wiki/Physical_body "Physical body")[[1]](#cite_note-1) under the influence of an attracting force. Alternatively, it is known as an **orbital revolution**, because it is a [rotation](/wiki/Rotation "Rotation") around an axis external to the moving body. Examples for orbits include the trajectory of a [planet](/wiki/Planet "Planet") around a star, a [natural satellite](/wiki/Natural_satellite "Natural satellite") around a planet, or an [artificial satellite](/wiki/Satellite "Satellite") around an object or position in space such as a planet, moon, asteroid, or [Lagrange point](/wiki/Lagrange_point "Lagrange point"). Normally, orbit refers to a regularly repeating trajectory, although it may also refer to a non-repeating trajectory. To a close approximation, planets, and satellites follow [elliptic orbits](/wiki/Elliptic_orbit "Elliptic orbit"), with the [center of mass](/wiki/Barycenter "Barycenter") being orbited at a focal point of the ellipse,[[2]](#cite_note-auto-2) as described by [Kepler's laws of planetary motion](/wiki/Kepler%27s_laws_of_planetary_motion "Kepler's laws of planetary motion").

Curved path of an object around a point

This article is about orbits in celestial mechanics, due to gravity. For other uses, see [Orbit (disambiguation)](/wiki/Orbit_(disambiguation) "Orbit (disambiguation)").

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/f/fa/Animation_of_Orbital_eccentricity.gif/250px-Animation_of_Orbital_eccentricity.gif)](/wiki/File:Animation_of_Orbital_eccentricity.gif)

Variation of orbital eccentricity  
  0.0   0.2   0.4   0.6   0.8

In [celestial mechanics](/wiki/Celestial_mechanics "Celestial mechanics"), an **orbit** is the curved [trajectory](/wiki/Trajectory "Trajectory") of an [object](/wiki/Physical_body "Physical body")[[1]](#cite_note-1) under the influence of an attracting force. Alternatively, it is known as an **orbital revolution**, because it is a [rotation](/wiki/Rotation "Rotation") around an axis external to the moving body. Examples for orbits include the trajectory of a [planet](/wiki/Planet "Planet") around a star, a [natural satellite](/wiki/Natural_satellite "Natural satellite") around a planet, or an [artificial satellite](/wiki/Satellite "Satellite") around an object or position in space such as a planet, moon, asteroid, or [Lagrange point](/wiki/Lagrange_point "Lagrange point"). Normally, orbit refers to a regularly repeating trajectory, although it may also refer to a non-repeating trajectory. To a close approximation, planets, and satellites follow [elliptic orbits](/wiki/Elliptic_orbit "Elliptic orbit"), with the [center of mass](/wiki/Barycenter "Barycenter") being orbited at a focal point of the ellipse,[[2]](#cite_note-auto-2) as described by [Kepler's laws of planetary motion](/wiki/Kepler%27s_laws_of_planetary_motion "Kepler's laws of planetary motion").

[Planets](/wiki/Planet "Planet") revolve around a star, a [natural satellite](/wiki/Natural_satellite "Natural satellite") around a planet, or an [artificial satellite](/wiki/Satellite "Satellite") around an object or position in space such as a planet, moon, asteroid, or [Lagrange point](/wiki/Lagrange_point "Lagrange point"). Normally, orbit refers to a regularly repeating trajectory, although it may also refer to a non-repeating trajectory. Furthermore, orbits are dynamic, [perturbated](/wiki/Perturbation_(astronomy) "Perturbation (astronomy)") by all masses, consisting of different [trajectories](/wiki/Trajectory "Trajectory"), but most can be approximated as [elliptic orbits](/wiki/Elliptic_orbit "Elliptic orbit"), with the barycenter being orbited at a focal point of the ellipse,[[2]](#cite_note-auto-2) as described by [Kepler's laws of planetary motion](/wiki/Kepler%27s_laws_of_planetary_motion "Kepler's laws of planetary motion").

For most situations, orbital motion is adequately approximated by [Newtonian mechanics](/wiki/Newtonian_mechanics "Newtonian mechanics"), which explains [gravity](/wiki/Newton%27s_law_of_universal_gravitation "Newton's law of universal gravitation") as a force obeying an [inverse-square law](/wiki/Inverse-square_law "Inverse-square law").[[3]](#cite_note-3) However, [Albert Einstein](/wiki/Albert_Einstein "Albert Einstein")'s [general theory of relativity](/wiki/General_theory_of_relativity "General theory of relativity"), which accounts for gravity as due to curvature of [spacetime](/wiki/Spacetime "Spacetime"), with orbits following [geodesics](/wiki/Geodesic "Geodesic"), provides a more accurate calculation and understanding of the exact mechanics of orbital motion.

## History

[![Andreas Cellarius, a Dutch mathematician, and geographer in the 17th century, compiled a celestial atlas with theories from astronomers like Ptolemy, and Copernicus. This illustration shows the Earth at the center, with the Moon, and planets orbiting around it, based on Ptolemy's geocentric model before Copernicus' heliocentric model.](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/2/28/Planisphaerium_Ptolemaicum_siue_machina_orbium_mundi_ex_hypothesi_Ptolemaica_in_plano_disposita_%282709983277%29.jpg/250px-Planisphaerium_Ptolemaicum_siue_machina_orbium_mundi_ex_hypothesi_Ptolemaica_in_plano_disposita_%282709983277%29.jpg)](/wiki/File:Planisphaerium_Ptolemaicum_siue_machina_orbium_mundi_ex_hypothesi_Ptolemaica_in_plano_disposita_(2709983277).jpg)

The Earth-centered universe according to Ptolemy; illustration by [Andreas Cellarius](/wiki/Andreas_Cellarius "Andreas Cellarius") from *[Harmonia Macrocosmica](/wiki/Harmonia_Macrocosmica "Harmonia Macrocosmica")*, 1660

Historically, the apparent motions of the planets were described by European, and Arabic philosophers using the idea of [celestial spheres](/wiki/Celestial_spheres "Celestial spheres"). This model posited the existence of perfect moving spheres or rings to which the stars and planets were attached. It assumed the heavens were fixed apart from the motion of the spheres and was developed without any understanding of gravity. This concept originated with [Hellenistic astronomy](/wiki/Hellenistic_astronomy "Hellenistic astronomy"), particularly [Eudoxus](/wiki/Eudoxus_of_Cnidus "Eudoxus of Cnidus") and [Aristotle](/wiki/Aristotle "Aristotle"). After the planets' motions were more accurately measured, theoretical mechanisms such as [deferent and epicycles](/wiki/Deferent_and_epicycle "Deferent and epicycle") were added by [Ptolemy](/wiki/Ptolemy "Ptolemy").[[4]](#cite_note-4) Although the model was capable of reasonably accurately predicting the planets' positions in the sky, more, and more epicycles were required as the measurements became more accurate, hence the model became increasingly unwieldy.[[5]](#cite_note-5) Originally [geocentric](/wiki/Geocentric_model "Geocentric model"), it was modified by [Copernicus](/wiki/Copernicus "Copernicus") to place the Sun at the centre to help simplify the model. The model was further challenged during the 16th century, as comets were observed traversing the spheres.[[6]](#cite_note-6)[[7]](#cite_note-7)

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/b/be/Solar_system_orbital_period_vs_semimajor_axis.svg/250px-Solar_system_orbital_period_vs_semimajor_axis.svg.png)](/wiki/File:Solar_system_orbital_period_vs_semimajor_axis.svg)

Distance from Sun vs. orbital period for Solar System bodies. Each object lies along the same line because the Sun has a much higher mass.

The basis for the modern description of orbits was first formulated by [Johannes Kepler](/wiki/Johannes_Kepler "Johannes Kepler") whose results are summarised in his three laws of planetary motion. First, he found that the orbits of the planets in the [Solar System](/wiki/Solar_System "Solar System") are elliptical, not [circular](/wiki/Circle "Circle") (or [epicyclic](/wiki/Epicycle "Epicycle")), as had previously been believed, and that the Sun is not located at the center of the orbits, but rather at one [focus](/wiki/Focus_(geometry) "Focus (geometry)"). Second, he found that the orbital speed of each planet is not constant, as had previously been thought, but rather that the speed depends on the planet's distance from the Sun. Third, Kepler found a universal relationship between the orbital properties of all the planets orbiting the Sun. For the planets, the cubes of their distances from the Sun are proportional to the squares of their orbital periods.[[8]](#cite_note-Kepler's_Laws_of_Planetary_Motion-8)

Jupiter and Venus, for example, are respectively about 5.2 and 0.723 [AU](/wiki/Astronomical_unit "Astronomical unit") distant from the Sun, their orbital periods respectively about 11.86 and 0.615 years. The proportionality is seen by the fact that the ratio for Jupiter:[[9]](#cite_note-9)

:   ![{\textstyle {\tfrac {5.204^{3}}{11.862^{2}}}\approxeq 1.002}](https://wikimedia.org/api/rest_v1/media/math/render/svg/71a47fda839dcc1ff663aff0d02224ada1d7b4d0)

is practically equal to that for Venus,[[10]](#cite_note-10)

:   ![{\textstyle {\tfrac {0.723^{3}}{0.615^{2}}}\approxeq 0.999}](https://wikimedia.org/api/rest_v1/media/math/render/svg/8b7dcb3203484d4ff47f0efc27655d2c5794874f)

in accord with the relationship. Idealised orbits meeting these rules are known as [Kepler orbits](/wiki/Kepler_orbits "Kepler orbits").

[Isaac Newton](/wiki/Isaac_Newton "Isaac Newton") demonstrated that Kepler's laws were derivable from his theory of [gravitation](/wiki/Gravitation "Gravitation"),[[11]](#cite_note-11) and that, in general, the orbits of bodies subject to gravity were [conic sections](/wiki/Conic_section "Conic section"),[[12]](#cite_note-12) under his assumption that the force of gravity propagates instantaneously.[[13]](#cite_note-Kembhavi_Khare_2020-13) To satisfy Kepler's third law, Newton showed that, for a pair of bodies, the orbit size (*a*), [orbital period](/wiki/Orbital_period "Orbital period") (*T*), and their combined masses (*M*) are related to each other by:[[14]](#cite_note-14)

:   ![{\displaystyle T^{2}\propto {\frac {a^{3}}{M}}}](https://wikimedia.org/api/rest_v1/media/math/render/svg/63e6196401046c5cf564ab2dc4862ea8370e612d)

and that those bodies orbit their common [center of mass](/wiki/Center_of_mass "Center of mass").[[15]](#cite_note-15) Where one body is much more massive than the other (as is the case of an artificial satellite orbiting a planet), it is a convenient approximation to take the center of mass as coinciding with the center of the more massive body.

Advances in Newtonian mechanics were then used to explore variations from the simple assumptions behind Kepler orbits, such as the perturbations due to other bodies, or the impact of spheroidal rather than spherical bodies. [Joseph-Louis Lagrange](/wiki/Joseph-Louis_Lagrange "Joseph-Louis Lagrange") developed a [new approach](/wiki/Lagrangian_mechanics "Lagrangian mechanics") to [Newtonian mechanics](/wiki/Newtonian_mechanics "Newtonian mechanics") emphasizing energy more than force,[[16]](#cite_note-16) and made progress on the [three-body problem](/wiki/Three-body_problem "Three-body problem"), discovering the [Lagrangian points](/wiki/Lagrangian_points "Lagrangian points") with [Euler](/wiki/Leonhard_Euler "Leonhard Euler").[[17]](#cite_note-17) In a dramatic vindication of classical mechanics, in 1846 [Urbain Le Verrier](/wiki/Urbain_Le_Verrier "Urbain Le Verrier") was able to predict the position of [Neptune](/wiki/Neptune "Neptune") based on unexplained perturbations in the orbit of [Uranus](/wiki/Uranus "Uranus").[[18]](#cite_note-18)

[Albert Einstein](/wiki/Albert_Einstein "Albert Einstein") in his 1916 paper *The Foundation of the General Theory of Relativity* explained that gravity was due to curvature of [space-time](/wiki/Space-time "Space-time") and removed Newton's assumption that changes in gravity propagate instantaneously. This led astronomers to recognize that Newtonian mechanics did not provide the highest accuracy in understanding orbits. In [relativity theory](/wiki/Relativity_theory "Relativity theory"), orbits follow geodesic trajectories which are usually approximated very well by the Newtonian predictions (except where there are very strong gravity fields and very high speeds) but the differences are measurable. Essentially all the experimental evidence that can distinguish between the theories agrees with relativity theory to within experimental measurement accuracy.[[13]](#cite_note-Kembhavi_Khare_2020-13) The original vindication of general relativity is that it was able to account for the remaining unexplained amount in [precession of Mercury's perihelion](/wiki/Tests_of_general_relativity#Perihelion_precession_of_Mercury "Tests of general relativity") first noted by Le Verrier.[[19]](#cite_note-O'Donnell_2014-19) However, Newton's solution is still used for most short term purposes since it is significantly easier to use, and sufficiently accurate.[[13]](#cite_note-Kembhavi_Khare_2020-13)

## Planetary orbits

[![Looping curve passing into and out of the circular Sun, marked with yearly intervals](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/2/25/SunAroundBarycenterXY.svg/250px-SunAroundBarycenterXY.svg.png)](/wiki/File:SunAroundBarycenterXY.svg)

Motion of the barycenter of the Solar System relative to the Sun, 2000–2050

[![An ellipse with the apsides marked at the extreme ends. The Sun at the ellipse focus closest to the perihelion](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/9/94/Perihelion-Aphelion.svg/250px-Perihelion-Aphelion.svg.png)](/wiki/File:Perihelion-Aphelion.svg)

The apsides of an object in an elliptical orbit with the Sun

Within a [planetary system](/wiki/Planetary_system "Planetary system"), various non-stellar objects follow [elliptical orbits](/wiki/Elliptical_orbit "Elliptical orbit") around the system's [barycenter](/wiki/Barycentric_coordinates_(astronomy) "Barycentric coordinates (astronomy)"). These objects include planets, [dwarf planets](/wiki/Dwarf_planet "Dwarf planet"), [asteroids](/wiki/Asteroid "Asteroid") and other [minor planets](/wiki/Minor_planet "Minor planet"), [comets](/wiki/Comet "Comet"), [meteoroids](/wiki/Meteoroid "Meteoroid"), and even [space debris](/wiki/Space_debris "Space debris").[[20]](#cite_note-20) A comet in a [parabolic](/wiki/Parabolic_trajectory "Parabolic trajectory") or [hyperbolic](/wiki/Hyperbolic_trajectory "Hyperbolic trajectory") orbit about a barycenter is not gravitationally bound to the star and therefore is not considered part of the star's planetary system.[[21]](#cite_note-21) Bodies that are gravitationally bound to one of the planets in a planetary system, including [natural satellites](/wiki/Natural_satellite "Natural satellite"), [artificial satellites](/wiki/Satellite "Satellite"), and the objects within [ring systems](/wiki/Ring_system "Ring system"), follow orbits about a barycenter near or within that planet.[[22]](#cite_note-Hahn_2020-22)

Owing to mutual [gravitational perturbations](/wiki/Perturbation_(astronomy) "Perturbation (astronomy)"), the [eccentricities](/wiki/Eccentricity_(orbit) "Eccentricity (orbit)") and [inclinations](/wiki/Orbital_inclination "Orbital inclination") of the planetary orbits vary over time.[[23]](#cite_note-23) [Mercury](/wiki/Mercury_(planet) "Mercury (planet)"), the smallest planet in the Solar System, has the most eccentric orbit. At the present [epoch](/wiki/Epoch_(astronomy) "Epoch (astronomy)"), [Mars](/wiki/Mars "Mars") has the next largest eccentricity while the smallest orbital eccentricities are seen with [Venus](/wiki/Venus "Venus") and [Neptune](/wiki/Neptune "Neptune").[[24]](#cite_note-24)

As two objects orbit each other, the [periapsis](/wiki/Periapsis "Periapsis") is that point at which the two objects are closest to each other. Less properly, "perifocus"[*[citation needed](/wiki/Wikipedia:Citation_needed "Wikipedia:Citation needed")*] or "pericentron" are used.[[25]](#cite_note-Wells_1965-25) The [apoapsis](/wiki/Apoapsis "Apoapsis") is that point at which they are the farthest, or sometimes apifocus[*[citation needed](/wiki/Wikipedia:Citation_needed "Wikipedia:Citation needed")*] or apocentron.[[25]](#cite_note-Wells_1965-25) A line drawn from periapsis to apoapsis is the *[line-of-apsides](/wiki/Line_of_apsides "Line of apsides")*. This is the major axis of the ellipse, the line through its longest part.[[26]](#cite_note-26)

More specific terms are used for specific bodies. For example, *perigee* and *apogee* are the lowest and highest parts of an orbit around Earth, while *perihelion* and *aphelion* are the closest and farthest points of an orbit around the Sun.[[27]](#cite_note-González–López_2025-27) Things orbiting the [Moon](/wiki/Moon "Moon") have a [perilune](/wiki/Perilune "Perilune") and [apolune](/wiki/Apolune "Apolune") (or [periselene](/wiki/Periselene "Periselene") and [aposelene](/wiki/Aposelene "Aposelene") respectively).[[28]](#cite_note-28) An orbit around any [star](/wiki/Star "Star"), not just the Sun, has a [periastron](/wiki/Periastron "Periastron") and an [apastron](/wiki/Apastron "Apastron").[[27]](#cite_note-González–López_2025-27)

In the case of planets orbiting a star, the mass of the star, and all its satellites are calculated to be at a single point called the barycenter. The individual satellites of that star follow their own elliptical orbits with the barycenter at one focal point of that ellipse.[[22]](#cite_note-Hahn_2020-22) At any point along its orbit, any satellite will have a certain value of kinetic and potential energy with respect to the barycenter and the sum of those two energies is a constant value at every point along its orbit. As a result, as a planet approaches periapsis, the planet will increase in speed as its potential energy decreases; as a planet approaches apoapsis, its velocity will decrease as its potential energy increases.[[29]](#cite_note-29)

## Principles

An orbit can be explained by combining [Newton's laws of motion](/wiki/Newton%27s_laws_of_motion "Newton's laws of motion") with his [law of universal gravitation](/wiki/Newton%27s_law_of_universal_gravitation "Newton's law of universal gravitation"). The laws of motion are as follows:[[30]](#cite_note-Newton_Oxford-30)

* A body continues in a state of uniform rest or motion unless acted upon by an external force.
* The acceleration produced when a force acts is directly proportional to the force and takes place in the direction in which the force acts.
* To every action there is an equal and opposite reaction.

By the first law of motion, in the absence of gravity, a physical object will continue to move in a straight line due to [inertia](/wiki/Inertia "Inertia"). According to the second law, a force, such as gravity, pulls the moving object toward the body that is the source of the force, and thus causes the object to follow a curved [trajectory](/wiki/Trajectory "Trajectory"). If the object has enough [tangential velocity](/wiki/Tangential_velocity "Tangential velocity"), it will not fall into the gravitating body but can instead continue to follow the curved trajectory caused by the force indefinitely. The object is then said to be orbiting the body. According to the third law, each body applies an equal force on the other, which means the two bodies orbit around their center of mass, or barycenter.[[31]](#cite_note-Arfken_2012-31)

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/5/59/Gravity_assist_still_Jupiter.svg/250px-Gravity_assist_still_Jupiter.svg.png)](/wiki/File:Gravity_assist_still_Jupiter.svg)

A [gravity assist](/wiki/Gravity_assist "Gravity assist") fly-by uses a hyperbolic orbit to change a spacecraft's velocity and heading[[32]](#cite_note-32)

Because of the law of universal gravitation, the strength of the gravitational force depends on the masses of the two bodies, and their separation. As the gravity varies over the course of the orbit, it reproduces Kepler's laws of planetary motion.[[31]](#cite_note-Arfken_2012-31) Depending on the evolving energy state of the system, the velocity relationship of two moving objects with mass can be considered in four practical classes, with subtypes:

No orbit

[Suborbital trajectories](/wiki/Sub-orbital_spaceflight "Sub-orbital spaceflight")
:   a range of interrupted elliptical paths

Orbital trajectories (or simply, orbits)
:   * Range of elliptical paths with closest point opposite firing point
    * Circular path
    * Range of elliptical paths with closest point at firing point

[Open (or escape) trajectories](/wiki/Escape_orbit "Escape orbit")
:   * Parabolic paths
    * Hyperbolic paths

To achieve orbit, conventional rockets are launched vertically at first to lift the rocket above the dense lower atmosphere (which causes frictional drag), and gradually pitch over and finish firing the rocket engine parallel to the atmosphere to achieve [orbital injection](/wiki/Orbital_injection "Orbital injection").[[33]](#cite_note-33) Once in orbit, their speed keeps them above the atmosphere. If an elliptical orbit dips into dense air, the object will lose speed, and re-enter, falling to the ground. Occasionally a space craft will intentionally intercept the atmosphere, in an act commonly referred to as an [aerobraking](/wiki/Aerobraking "Aerobraking") maneuver.[[34]](#cite_note-34)

### Illustration

Main article: [Newton's cannonball](/wiki/Newton%27s_cannonball "Newton's cannonball")

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/7/73/Newton_Cannon.svg/330px-Newton_Cannon.svg.png)](/wiki/File:Newton_Cannon.svg)

[Newton's cannonball](/wiki/Newton%27s_cannonball "Newton's cannonball"), an illustration of how objects can "fall" in a curve

As an illustration of an orbit around a planet, the [Newton's cannonball](/wiki/Newton%27s_cannonball "Newton's cannonball") model may prove useful (see image). This is a '[thought experiment](/wiki/Thought_experiment "Thought experiment")', in which a cannon on top of a tall mountain is able to fire a cannonball horizontally at any chosen muzzle speed. The effects of air friction on the cannonball are ignored (or perhaps the mountain is high enough that the cannon is above the Earth's atmosphere, which is the same thing).[[35]](#cite_note-35)

If the cannon fires its ball with a low initial speed, the trajectory of the ball curves downward, and hits the ground (A). As the firing speed is increased, the cannonball hits the ground farther (B) away from the cannon, because while the ball is still falling towards the ground, the ground is increasingly curving away from it (see first point, above). All these motions are actually "orbits" in a technical sense—they are describing a portion of an elliptical path around the center of gravity—but the orbits are interrupted by striking the Earth.

If the cannonball is fired with sufficient speed, the ground curves away from the ball at least as much as the ball falls—so the ball never strikes the ground. It is now in what could be called a non-interrupted or circumnavigating, orbit. For any specific combination of height above the center of gravity and mass of the planet, there is one specific firing speed (unaffected by the mass of the ball, which is assumed to be very small relative to the Earth's mass) that produces a [circular orbit](/wiki/Circular_orbit "Circular orbit"), as shown in (C).

As the firing speed is increased beyond this, non-interrupted elliptic orbits are produced; one is shown in (D). If the initial firing is above the surface of the Earth as shown, there will also be non-interrupted elliptical orbits at slower firing speed; these will come closest to the Earth at the point half an orbit beyond, and directly opposite the firing point, below the circular orbit.

At a specific horizontal firing speed called [escape velocity](/wiki/Escape_velocity "Escape velocity"), dependent on the mass of the planet and the distance of the object from the barycenter, an open orbit (E) is achieved that has a [parabolic path](/wiki/Parabolic_trajectory "Parabolic trajectory"). At even greater speeds the object will follow a range of [hyperbolic trajectories](/wiki/Hyperbolic_trajectory "Hyperbolic trajectory"). In a practical sense, both of these trajectory types mean the object is "breaking free" of the planet's gravity, and "going off into space" potentially never to return. However, the object remains under the influence of the Sun's gravity.[[36]](#cite_note-36)

## Newton's laws

### Gravity and motion

In most real-world situations, [Newton's laws](/wiki/Newton%27s_laws "Newton's laws") provide a reasonably accurate description of motion of objects in a gravitational field. The adjustments needed to accommodate the [theory of relativity](/wiki/Theory_of_relativity "Theory of relativity") become appreciable in cases where the object is in the proximity of a significant gravitational source such as a star,[[37]](#cite_note-37) or a high level of accuracy is needed.

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/3/34/Gravity_turn_in_tangential_axes.svg/250px-Gravity_turn_in_tangential_axes.svg.png)](/wiki/File:Gravity_turn_in_tangential_axes.svg)

A rocket experiences a gravitational force *g* and acceleration from propulsion *ae*, resulting in a net acceleration *a*.

The acceleration of a body is equal to the combination of the forces acting on it, divided by its mass. The gravitational force acting on a body is proportional to the product of the masses of the two attracting bodies and decreases inversely with the square of the distance between them.[[31]](#cite_note-Arfken_2012-31) For a [two-body problem](/wiki/Two-body_problem "Two-body problem"), defined as an isolated system of two spherical bodies with known masses and sufficient separation, this Newtonian approximation of their gravitational interaction can provide a reasonably accurate calculation of their trajectories.[[38]](#cite_note-38)

If the heavier body is much more massive than the smaller, as in the case of a satellite or small moon orbiting a planet or for the Earth orbiting the Sun, it is accurate enough, and convenient to describe the motion in terms of a [coordinate system](/wiki/Coordinate_system "Coordinate system") that is centered on the heavier body, and we say that the lighter body is in orbit around the heavier. For the case where the masses of two bodies are comparable, an exact Newtonian solution is still sufficient, and can be had by placing the coordinate system at the center of the mass of the system.[[39]](#cite_note-39)

### Energy and conic sections

Energy is associated with [gravitational fields](/wiki/Gravitational_field "Gravitational field"). A stationary body far from another can do external work if it is pulled towards it, and therefore has gravitational *[potential energy](/wiki/Potential_energy "Potential energy")*. Since work is required to separate two bodies against the pull of gravity, their gravitational potential energy increases as they are separated and decreases as they approach one another. For point masses, the gravitational energy decreases to zero as they approach zero separation. It is convenient and conventional to assign the potential energy as having zero value when they are an infinite distance apart, and hence it has a negative value (since it decreases from zero) for smaller finite distances.[[40]](#cite_note-40)

When only two gravitational bodies interact, their orbits follow a [conic section](/wiki/Conic_section "Conic section"). The orbit can be open (implying the object never returns) or closed (returning). Which it is depends on the total [energy](/wiki/Energy "Energy") ([kinetic](/wiki/Kinetic_energy "Kinetic energy") + [potential energy](/wiki/Potential_energy "Potential energy")) of the system. In the case of an open orbit, the speed at any position of the orbit is at least the [escape velocity](/wiki/Escape_velocity "Escape velocity") for that position, in the case of a closed orbit, the speed is always less than the escape velocity. Since the kinetic energy is never negative if the common convention is adopted of taking the potential energy as zero at infinite separation, the bound orbits will have negative total energy, the parabolic trajectories zero total energy and hyperbolic orbits positive total energy.[[41]](#cite_note-Curtis_2023-41)[[42]](#cite_note-Spencer_Conte_2023-42)

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/1/11/Conic_Sections.svg/250px-Conic_Sections.svg.png)](/wiki/File:Conic_Sections.svg)

Conic sections for different orbit types

An open orbit will have a parabolic shape if it has the velocity of exactly the escape velocity at that point in its trajectory, and it will have the shape of a [hyperbola](/wiki/Hyperbola "Hyperbola") when its velocity is greater than the escape velocity.[[41]](#cite_note-Curtis_2023-41)[[42]](#cite_note-Spencer_Conte_2023-42) When two bodies approach each other with escape velocity or greater (relative to each other), they will briefly curve around each other at the time of their closest approach, and then separate, and fly apart.

All closed orbits have the shape of an [ellipse](/wiki/Ellipse "Ellipse"). A circular orbit is a special case, wherein the foci of the ellipse coincide.[[41]](#cite_note-Curtis_2023-41)[[42]](#cite_note-Spencer_Conte_2023-42)

### Kepler's laws

Bodies following closed orbits repeat their paths with a certain time called the period. This motion is described by the empirical laws of Kepler, which can be mathematically derived from Newton's laws. These can be formulated as follows:[[43]](#cite_note-NASA_Kepler_2024-43)

1. The orbit of a planet around the [Sun](/wiki/Sun "Sun") is an ellipse, with the Sun in one of the focal points of that ellipse. [This focal point is actually the [barycenter](/wiki/Barycenter "Barycenter") of the [Sun-planet system](/wiki/Solar_System "Solar System"); for simplicity, this explanation assumes the Sun's mass is infinitely larger than that planet's.] The planet's orbit lies in a plane, called the **[orbital plane](/wiki/Orbital_plane_(astronomy) "Orbital plane (astronomy)")**.[[43]](#cite_note-NASA_Kepler_2024-43)
2. As the planet moves in its orbit, the line from the Sun to the planet sweeps a constant area of the [orbital plane](/wiki/Orbital_plane_(astronomy) "Orbital plane (astronomy)") for a given period of time, regardless of which part of its orbit the planet traces during that period of time. This means that the planet moves faster near its [perihelion](/wiki/Perihelion "Perihelion") than near its [aphelion](/wiki/Aphelion "Aphelion"), because at the smaller distance it needs to trace a greater arc to cover the same area.[[43]](#cite_note-NASA_Kepler_2024-43) This law is usually stated as "equal areas in equal time."
3. For a given orbit, the ratio of the cube of its [semi-major axis](/wiki/Semi-major_axis "Semi-major axis") to the square of its period is constant.[[43]](#cite_note-NASA_Kepler_2024-43)

### Limitations of classical mechanics

Ideally, the bound orbits of a point mass or a spherical body with a [Newtonian gravitational field](/wiki/Newtonian_gravitational_field "Newtonian gravitational field") form closed [ellipses](/wiki/Ellipse "Ellipse"), which repeat the same path exactly, and indefinitely. However, any non-spherical or non-Newtonian effects will cause the orbit's shape to depart from the ellipse. Such effects can be caused by a slight [oblateness](/wiki/Flattening "Flattening") of the body,[[44]](#cite_note-44) [mass anomalies](/wiki/Mass_concentration_(astronomy) "Mass concentration (astronomy)"),[[45]](#cite_note-45) [tidal](/wiki/Tide "Tide") deformations,[[46]](#cite_note-46) or [relativistic effects](/wiki/Theory_of_relativity "Theory of relativity"),[[19]](#cite_note-O'Donnell_2014-19) thereby changing the gravitational field's behavior with distance.

The two-body solutions were published by Newton in [Principia](/wiki/Philosophiae_Naturalis_Principia_Mathematica "Philosophiae Naturalis Principia Mathematica") in 1687.[[30]](#cite_note-Newton_Oxford-30) In 1912, [Karl Fritiof Sundman](/wiki/Karl_Fritiof_Sundman "Karl Fritiof Sundman") developed a converging infinite series that solves the general [three-body problem](/wiki/Three-body_problem "Three-body problem"); however, it converges too slowly to be of much use. The restricted three-body problem, in which the third body is assumed to have negligible mass, has been extensively studied. The solutions to this case include the [Lagrangian points](/wiki/Lagrangian_point "Lagrangian point").[[47]](#cite_note-Karttunen_et_al_2007-47) In the case of [lunar theory](/wiki/Lunar_theory "Lunar theory"), the 19th century work of [Charles-Eugène Delaunay](/wiki/Charles-Eug%C3%A8ne_Delaunay "Charles-Eugène Delaunay") allowed the motions of the Moon to be predicted to within its own diameter over a 20-year period.[[48]](#cite_note-48) No universally valid method is known to solve the equations of motion for a system with four or more bodies.

## Formulation

Main article: [Orbit modeling](/wiki/Orbit_modeling "Orbit modeling")

### Newtonian analysis of orbital motion

Further information: [Kepler orbit](/wiki/Kepler_orbit "Kepler orbit"), [orbit equation](/wiki/Orbit_equation "Orbit equation"), and [Kepler's first law](/wiki/Kepler%27s_first_law "Kepler's first law")

The following derivation applies to an elliptical orbit. The assumption is that the central body is massive enough that it can be considered to be stationary and so the more subtle effects of [general relativity](/wiki/General_relativity "General relativity") can be ignored.

#### Force and acceleration

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/0/0e/NewtonsLawOfUniversalGravitation.svg/250px-NewtonsLawOfUniversalGravitation.svg.png)](/wiki/File:NewtonsLawOfUniversalGravitation.svg)

Gravitational force for masses *m*1 and *m*2 with separation *r*

The [Newtonian](/wiki/Classical_mechanics "Classical mechanics") law of gravitation states that the gravitational acceleration of the second mass towards the central body is related to the inverse of the square of the distance between them, namely:[[49]](#cite_note-Fitzpatrick_2023-49)

:   ![{\displaystyle F_{2}=-{\frac {Gm_{1}m_{2}}{r^{2}}}}](https://wikimedia.org/api/rest_v1/media/math/render/svg/7c3165c7494ba1de97468bdfcaa3291db51684eb)

where *F*2 is the force acting on the mass *m*2 caused by the gravitational attraction mass *m*1 has for *m*2, *G* is the universal [gravitational constant](/wiki/Gravitational_constant "Gravitational constant"), and *r* is the distance between the two masses centers.

From Newton's second law, the summation of the forces acting on *m*2 related to that body's acceleration:

:   ![{\displaystyle F_{2}=m_{2}A_{2}}](https://wikimedia.org/api/rest_v1/media/math/render/svg/dba8434884436bb09347c0bd46294e55fc5831ad)

where *A*2 is the acceleration of *m*2 caused by the force of gravitational attraction *F*2 of *m*1 acting on *m*2.

Combining Eq. 1 and 2:

:   ![{\displaystyle -{\frac {Gm_{1}m_{2}}{r^{2}}}=m_{2}A_{2}}](https://wikimedia.org/api/rest_v1/media/math/render/svg/6924dd36d2f574232731b6ae605a86bd151f01be)

Solving for the acceleration, *A*2:

:   ![{\displaystyle A_{2}={\frac {F_{2}}{m_{2}}}=-{\frac {1}{m_{2}}}{\frac {Gm_{1}m_{2}}{r^{2}}}=-{\frac {\mu }{r^{2}}}}](https://wikimedia.org/api/rest_v1/media/math/render/svg/e0b325ec1a8f813c5e6aff5e888dde2a076d8a6c)

where ![{\displaystyle \mu \,}](https://wikimedia.org/api/rest_v1/media/math/render/svg/8d20addf0d9f04e185714134b97726c4bf17d340) is the [standard gravitational parameter](/wiki/Standard_gravitational_parameter "Standard gravitational parameter"), in this case ![{\displaystyle Gm_{1}}](https://wikimedia.org/api/rest_v1/media/math/render/svg/7e8991b9e06ab9fbe4a0f79da48bca1db4364d0d).[[50]](#cite_note-50) It is understood that the system being described is *m*2, hence the subscripts can be dropped.

#### Polar coordinates

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/e/e8/Polar_coordinates_with_unit_vectors.svg/250px-Polar_coordinates_with_unit_vectors.svg.png)](/wiki/File:Polar_coordinates_with_unit_vectors.svg)

Unit vectors in polar coordinates

The position of the orbiting object at the current time ![{\displaystyle t}](https://wikimedia.org/api/rest_v1/media/math/render/svg/65658b7b223af9e1acc877d848888ecdb4466560) is located in the [orbital plane](/wiki/Orbital_plane "Orbital plane") using [vector calculus](/wiki/Vector_calculus "Vector calculus") in [polar coordinates](/wiki/Polar_coordinates "Polar coordinates"), both with the standard Euclidean [basis](/wiki/Basis_(linear_algebra) "Basis (linear algebra)") and with the polar basis with the origin coinciding with the center of force. Let ![{\displaystyle r}](https://wikimedia.org/api/rest_v1/media/math/render/svg/0d1ecb613aa2984f0576f70f86650b7c2a132538) be the distance between the object and the center and ![{\displaystyle \theta }](https://wikimedia.org/api/rest_v1/media/math/render/svg/6e5ab2664b422d53eb0c7df3b87e1360d75ad9af) be the angle it has rotated. Let ![{\displaystyle {\hat {\mathbf {x} }}}](https://wikimedia.org/api/rest_v1/media/math/render/svg/8a5451354f837d5d89774e1de386b44a903d929d) and ![{\displaystyle {\hat {\mathbf {y} }}}](https://wikimedia.org/api/rest_v1/media/math/render/svg/c3826fc591cfe42348f58b15e4c23152f30931ef) be the standard [Euclidean](/wiki/Euclidean_space "Euclidean space") bases and let:[[49]](#cite_note-Fitzpatrick_2023-49)

:   ![{\displaystyle {\hat {\mathbf {r} }}=\cos(\theta ){\hat {\mathbf {x} }}+\sin(\theta ){\hat {\mathbf {y} }}}](https://wikimedia.org/api/rest_v1/media/math/render/svg/e43843800f4be3f5f7be1274b135d5e61ccce974)
:   ![{\displaystyle {\hat {\boldsymbol {\theta }}}=-\sin(\theta ){\hat {\mathbf {x} }}+\cos(\theta ){\hat {\mathbf {y} }}}](https://wikimedia.org/api/rest_v1/media/math/render/svg/e24f0578060902f73bb3db70ab39a1e553de2f24)

be the radial and transverse [polar](/wiki/Polar_coordinate_system#Vector_calculus "Polar coordinate system") basis. The first is the [unit vector](/wiki/Unit_vector "Unit vector") pointing from the central body to the current location of the orbiting object and the second is the orthogonal unit vector pointing in the direction that the orbiting object would travel if orbiting in a counter clockwise circle. Then the vector to the orbiting object is:

:   ![{\displaystyle {\mathbf {O} }=r\cos(\theta ){\hat {\mathbf {x} }}+r\sin(\theta ){\hat {\mathbf {y} }}=r{\hat {\mathbf {r} }}}](https://wikimedia.org/api/rest_v1/media/math/render/svg/3b6675693afc53d69b74ecb570d97312eb5905eb)

[Newton's notation](/wiki/Notation_for_differentiation#Newton's_notation "Notation for differentiation") ![{\displaystyle {\dot {r}}}](https://wikimedia.org/api/rest_v1/media/math/render/svg/99b51a39764cf6daa615ef2144ac420ecff01cfc) and ![{\displaystyle {\dot {\theta }}}](https://wikimedia.org/api/rest_v1/media/math/render/svg/f78124b2aea53527d3e053cbbdd9c7ded2c8f05f) denotes the standard [derivatives](/wiki/Derivative "Derivative") of how this distance and angle change over time.[[51]](#cite_note-51) Taking the derivative of a vector to see how it changes over a tiny increment of time, ![{\displaystyle \delta t}](https://wikimedia.org/api/rest_v1/media/math/render/svg/eb5768e76c1d7222b1b53d613e8622471ef18327), subtracts its location at time ![{\displaystyle t+\delta t}](https://wikimedia.org/api/rest_v1/media/math/render/svg/9cf2cc78aea7996c669d0428babe7c88053ab22d) from that at time ![{\displaystyle t}](https://wikimedia.org/api/rest_v1/media/math/render/svg/65658b7b223af9e1acc877d848888ecdb4466560) and divides by ![{\displaystyle \delta t}](https://wikimedia.org/api/rest_v1/media/math/render/svg/eb5768e76c1d7222b1b53d613e8622471ef18327). The result remains a vector.

#### Kepler's second law

Because the basis vector ![{\displaystyle {\hat {\mathbf {r} }}}](https://wikimedia.org/api/rest_v1/media/math/render/svg/9740464b71653e12932278ee944540be8caa5b96) moves as the object orbits, the first step is to differentiate it to determine the radial rate of change with time. From time ![{\displaystyle t}](https://wikimedia.org/api/rest_v1/media/math/render/svg/65658b7b223af9e1acc877d848888ecdb4466560) to ![{\displaystyle t+\delta t}](https://wikimedia.org/api/rest_v1/media/math/render/svg/9cf2cc78aea7996c669d0428babe7c88053ab22d), the vector ![{\displaystyle {\hat {\mathbf {r} }}}](https://wikimedia.org/api/rest_v1/media/math/render/svg/9740464b71653e12932278ee944540be8caa5b96) keeps its beginning at the origin and rotates from angle ![{\displaystyle \theta }](https://wikimedia.org/api/rest_v1/media/math/render/svg/6e5ab2664b422d53eb0c7df3b87e1360d75ad9af) to ![{\displaystyle \theta +{\dot {\theta }}\ \delta t}](https://wikimedia.org/api/rest_v1/media/math/render/svg/9e43a1beeb2deb404ff0c6489faa0a8d7e20e1f2), which moves its head a distance ![{\displaystyle {\dot {\theta }}\ \delta t}](https://wikimedia.org/api/rest_v1/media/math/render/svg/4ca4f11e634480b3b07ec326cf3964d6e9fd0a9f) in the perpendicular direction ![{\displaystyle {\hat {\boldsymbol {\theta }}}}](https://wikimedia.org/api/rest_v1/media/math/render/svg/ca32f6d693b0a183a7728b875f7e2042bb1dbca2), giving a derivative of ![{\displaystyle {\dot {\theta }}{\hat {\boldsymbol {\theta }}}}](https://wikimedia.org/api/rest_v1/media/math/render/svg/89c2050809e39db493da13208ce78fa999d60905).

:   ![{\displaystyle {\begin{aligned}{\hat {\mathbf {r} }}&=\cos(\theta ){\hat {\mathbf {x} }}+\sin(\theta ){\hat {\mathbf {y} }}\\{\frac {\delta {\hat {\mathbf {r} }}}{\delta t}}={\dot {\mathbf {r} }}&=-\sin(\theta ){\dot {\theta }}{\hat {\mathbf {x} }}+\cos(\theta ){\dot {\theta }}{\hat {\mathbf {y} }}={\dot {\theta }}{\hat {\boldsymbol {\theta }}}\\{\hat {\boldsymbol {\theta }}}&=-\sin(\theta ){\hat {\mathbf {x} }}+\cos(\theta ){\hat {\mathbf {y} }}\\{\frac {\delta {\hat {\boldsymbol {\theta }}}}{\delta t}}={\dot {\boldsymbol {\theta }}}&=-\cos(\theta ){\dot {\theta }}{\hat {\mathbf {x} }}-\sin(\theta ){\dot {\theta }}{\hat {\mathbf {y} }}=-{\dot {\theta }}{\hat {\mathbf {r} }}\end{aligned}}}](https://wikimedia.org/api/rest_v1/media/math/render/svg/3baa9934c7e4e62e87b0d07d4d3eb8de62e08e08)

The velocity and acceleration of the orbiting object can now be determined.[[49]](#cite_note-Fitzpatrick_2023-49)

:   ![{\displaystyle {\begin{aligned}{\mathbf {O} }&=r{\hat {\mathbf {r} }}\\{\dot {\mathbf {O} }}&={\frac {\delta r}{\delta t}}{\hat {\mathbf {r} }}+r{\frac {\delta {\hat {\mathbf {r} }}}{\delta t}}={\dot {r}}{\hat {\mathbf {r} }}+r\left[{\dot {\theta }}{\hat {\boldsymbol {\theta }}}\right]\\{\ddot {\mathbf {O} }}&=\left[{\ddot {r}}{\hat {\mathbf {r} }}+{\dot {r}}{\dot {\theta }}{\hat {\boldsymbol {\theta }}}\right]+\left[{\dot {r}}{\dot {\theta }}{\hat {\boldsymbol {\theta }}}+r{\ddot {\theta }}{\hat {\boldsymbol {\theta }}}-r{\dot {\theta }}^{2}{\hat {\mathbf {r} }}\right]\\&=\left[{\ddot {r}}-r{\dot {\theta }}^{2}\right]{\hat {\mathbf {r} }}+\left[r{\ddot {\theta }}+2{\dot {r}}{\dot {\theta }}\right]{\hat {\boldsymbol {\theta }}}\end{aligned}}}](https://wikimedia.org/api/rest_v1/media/math/render/svg/b1b136e7bbdc0c47f80eb1d1b5600dfe2cf4f4d1)

In the last line, the coefficients of ![{\displaystyle {\hat {\mathbf {r} }}}](https://wikimedia.org/api/rest_v1/media/math/render/svg/9740464b71653e12932278ee944540be8caa5b96) and ![{\displaystyle {\hat {\boldsymbol {\theta }}}}](https://wikimedia.org/api/rest_v1/media/math/render/svg/ca32f6d693b0a183a7728b875f7e2042bb1dbca2) give the accelerations in the radial and transverse directions. As said, Newton gives the first due to gravity as ![{\displaystyle -\mu /r^{2}}](https://wikimedia.org/api/rest_v1/media/math/render/svg/cf627c13db72632020419c214834d8d1ecdcca70) and the second is zero per Newton's first law. Thus:[[49]](#cite_note-Fitzpatrick_2023-49)

|  |  |  |
| --- | --- | --- |
| {\displaystyle {\ddot {r}}-r{\dot {\theta }}^{2}=-{\frac {\mu }{r^{2}}}} |  | 1 |

|  |  |  |
| --- | --- | --- |
| {\displaystyle r{\ddot {\theta }}+2{\dot {r}}{\dot {\theta }}=0} |  | 2 |

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/2/27/Second_law_of_Kepler.svg/250px-Second_law_of_Kepler.svg.png)](/wiki/File:Second_law_of_Kepler.svg)

For a time interval *t*, the rate of angular change ![{\textstyle {\dot {\theta }}}](https://wikimedia.org/api/rest_v1/media/math/render/svg/d891343f186615b232240262bd52a79db5270450) varies to maintain a constant area *A*

Equation (2) can be rearranged using [integration by parts](/wiki/Integration_by_parts "Integration by parts").

:   ![{\displaystyle r{\ddot {\theta }}+2{\dot {r}}{\dot {\theta }}={\frac {1}{r}}{\frac {d}{dt}}\left(r^{2}{\dot {\theta }}\right)=0}](https://wikimedia.org/api/rest_v1/media/math/render/svg/cae737e0b7f29cae9b7ae88d1aaeac8b4c2e75c4)

Both sides can now be multiplied through by ![{\displaystyle r}](https://wikimedia.org/api/rest_v1/media/math/render/svg/0d1ecb613aa2984f0576f70f86650b7c2a132538) because it is not zero unless the orbiting object crashes. Having the derivative be zero indicates that the function is a constant.

|  |  |  |
| --- | --- | --- |
| {\displaystyle r^{2}{\dot {\theta }}=h} |  | 3 |

which is actually the theoretical proof of [Kepler's second law](/wiki/Kepler%27s_second_law "Kepler's second law") (A line joining a planet and the Sun sweeps out equal areas during equal intervals of time).[[49]](#cite_note-Fitzpatrick_2023-49) The [constant of integration](/wiki/Constant_of_integration "Constant of integration"), *h*, is the [angular momentum per unit mass](/wiki/Specific_relative_angular_momentum "Specific relative angular momentum").[[52]](#cite_note-52)

#### Kepler's first law

In order to get an equation for the orbit from equation (1), the time variable needs to be eliminated. (See also [Binet equation](/wiki/Binet_equation "Binet equation").)
In polar coordinates, this would express the distance ![{\displaystyle r}](https://wikimedia.org/api/rest_v1/media/math/render/svg/0d1ecb613aa2984f0576f70f86650b7c2a132538) of the orbiting object from the center as a function of its angle ![{\displaystyle \theta }](https://wikimedia.org/api/rest_v1/media/math/render/svg/6e5ab2664b422d53eb0c7df3b87e1360d75ad9af). However, it is easier to introduce the auxiliary variable ![{\displaystyle u=1/r}](https://wikimedia.org/api/rest_v1/media/math/render/svg/5a54441342a7391a1d75f9816d6aa16f1b72c150) and to express ![{\displaystyle u}](https://wikimedia.org/api/rest_v1/media/math/render/svg/c3e6bb763d22c20916ed4f0bb6bd49d7470cffd8) as a function of ![{\displaystyle \theta }](https://wikimedia.org/api/rest_v1/media/math/render/svg/6e5ab2664b422d53eb0c7df3b87e1360d75ad9af). Derivatives of ![{\displaystyle r}](https://wikimedia.org/api/rest_v1/media/math/render/svg/0d1ecb613aa2984f0576f70f86650b7c2a132538) with respect to time may be rewritten as derivatives of ![{\displaystyle u}](https://wikimedia.org/api/rest_v1/media/math/render/svg/c3e6bb763d22c20916ed4f0bb6bd49d7470cffd8) with respect to angle.[[49]](#cite_note-Fitzpatrick_2023-49)

:   ![{\displaystyle u={1 \over r}}](https://wikimedia.org/api/rest_v1/media/math/render/svg/edb030fe58ea5d65b793046e1fc6503b68f913de)
:   ![{\displaystyle {\dot {\theta }}={\frac {h}{r^{2}}}=hu^{2}}](https://wikimedia.org/api/rest_v1/media/math/render/svg/34c3839d0e74d320724bae5e68c218eeb1a0d062) (reworking (3))
:   ![{\displaystyle {\begin{aligned}{\frac {\delta u}{\delta \theta }}&={\frac {\delta }{\delta t}}\left({\frac {1}{r}}\right){\frac {\delta t}{\delta \theta }}=-{\frac {\dot {r}}{r^{2}{\dot {\theta }}}}=-{\frac {\dot {r}}{h}}\\{\frac {\delta ^{2}u}{\delta \theta ^{2}}}&=-{\frac {1}{h}}{\frac {\delta {\dot {r}}}{\delta t}}{\frac {\delta t}{\delta \theta }}=-{\frac {\ddot {r}}{h{\dot {\theta }}}}=-{\frac {\ddot {r}}{h^{2}u^{2}}}\ \ \ {\text{ or }}\ \ \ {\ddot {r}}=-h^{2}u^{2}{\frac {\delta ^{2}u}{\delta \theta ^{2}}}\end{aligned}}}](https://wikimedia.org/api/rest_v1/media/math/render/svg/9f09581310b50f8ae8a9ab58043a77b66b83f3c8)

Plugging these into (1) gives

:   ![{\displaystyle {\begin{aligned}{\ddot {r}}-r{\dot {\theta }}^{2}&=-{\frac {\mu }{r^{2}}}\\-h^{2}u^{2}{\frac {\delta ^{2}u}{\delta \theta ^{2}}}-{\frac {1}{u}}\left(hu^{2}\right)^{2}&=-\mu u^{2}\end{aligned}}}](https://wikimedia.org/api/rest_v1/media/math/render/svg/1773ecda9fe1421723149bdfac81d5693ec57bbc)

Hence:[[49]](#cite_note-Fitzpatrick_2023-49)

|  |  |  |
| --- | --- | --- |
| {\displaystyle {\frac {\delta ^{2}u}{\delta \theta ^{2}}}+u={\frac {\mu }{h^{2}}}} |  | 4 |

So for the gravitational force – or, more generally, for *any* inverse square force law – the right hand side of the equation becomes a constant and the equation is seen to be the [harmonic equation](/wiki/Harmonic_oscillator "Harmonic oscillator") (up to a shift of origin of the dependent variable). The solution is:

:   ![{\displaystyle u(\theta )={\frac {\mu }{h^{2}}}+A\cos(\theta -\theta _{0})}](https://wikimedia.org/api/rest_v1/media/math/render/svg/fab8ce40c503dcfb8c1009b878938e831b2b1ff3)

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/e/ef/Ellipse_Polar.svg/250px-Ellipse_Polar.svg.png)](/wiki/File:Ellipse_Polar.svg)

Polar coordinates centered at focus. The semi-major axis *a* is the distance from the center (C) to an apsis (A or B).

where *A* and *θ*0 are arbitrary constants. This resulting equation of the orbit of the object is that of an [ellipse in Polar form](/wiki/Ellipse#Polar_form_relative_to_focus "Ellipse") relative to one of the focal points. This is put into a more standard form by letting ![{\displaystyle e\equiv h^{2}A/\mu }](https://wikimedia.org/api/rest_v1/media/math/render/svg/1140979616948d79c62f66180b7f249f77dcaeff) be the [eccentricity](/wiki/Eccentricity_(orbit) "Eccentricity (orbit)"), which when rearranged we see:

:   ![{\displaystyle u(\theta )={\frac {\mu }{h^{2}}}(1+e\cos(\theta -\theta _{0}))}](https://wikimedia.org/api/rest_v1/media/math/render/svg/2db7995b7efe3bdc9dfb29de01807314a90be16c)

Note that by letting ![{\displaystyle a\equiv h^{2}/\mu \left(1-e^{2}\right)}](https://wikimedia.org/api/rest_v1/media/math/render/svg/8657654204e2f1f4cf11179f4dc74a12f8e8b336) be the semi-major axis and letting ![{\displaystyle \theta _{0}\equiv 0}](https://wikimedia.org/api/rest_v1/media/math/render/svg/272d6afef5d598d137d6e645b78f88e9ce5d92fe) so the long axis of the ellipse is along the positive *x* coordinate yields:[[49]](#cite_note-Fitzpatrick_2023-49)

:   ![{\displaystyle r(\theta )={\frac {a\left(1-e^{2}\right)}{1+e\cos \theta }}}](https://wikimedia.org/api/rest_v1/media/math/render/svg/378bfa0603892f4d8a8e1b01ecb17e93cc5f2949)

When *e* is zero, the result is a circular orbit with *r* equal to *a*.

#### Kepler's third law

Incorporating Newton's laws, the constant in Kepler's third law can be shown to be:[[53]](#cite_note-53)

:   ![{\displaystyle {\frac {a^{3}}{T^{2}}}={\frac {G(M+m)}{4\pi ^{2}}}\approx {\frac {GM}{4\pi ^{2}}}\approx 7.496\times 10^{-6}{\frac {{\text{AU}}^{3}}{{\text{days}}^{2}}}}](https://wikimedia.org/api/rest_v1/media/math/render/svg/dfb81e2d4350895c419c3713cabc163b1a5da3bf)

where ![{\displaystyle M}](https://wikimedia.org/api/rest_v1/media/math/render/svg/f82cade9898ced02fdd08712e5f0c0151758a0dd) is the [mass of the Sun](/wiki/Solar_mass "Solar mass"), *G* is the gravitational constant, ![{\displaystyle m}](https://wikimedia.org/api/rest_v1/media/math/render/svg/0a07d98bb302f3856cbabc47b2b9016692e3f7bc) is the mass of the planet, ![{\displaystyle T}](https://wikimedia.org/api/rest_v1/media/math/render/svg/ec7200acd984a1d3a3d7dc455e262fbe54f7f6e0) is the orbital period and ![{\displaystyle a}](https://wikimedia.org/api/rest_v1/media/math/render/svg/ffd2487510aa438433a2579450ab2b3d557e5edc) is the elliptical semi-major axis, and ![{\displaystyle {\text{AU}}}](https://wikimedia.org/api/rest_v1/media/math/render/svg/1fb03216995e17d94cadd0e00c661e3f88a213c1) is the [astronomical unit](/wiki/Astronomical_unit "Astronomical unit"), the average distance from earth to the sun. From this, the orbital period can be derived from the semi-major axis.

#### Applying torque

A torque to a satellite can result, for example, due to perturbation from a non-sperical mass.[[54]](#cite_note-54) When the two-body system is under the influence of torque, the angular momentum *h* is not a constant. After the following calculation:

The result is the [Sturm-Liouville equation](/wiki/Sturm-Liouville_equation "Sturm-Liouville equation") of two-body system.[[55]](#cite_note-55)
