### Relativistic orbital motion

The classical ([Newtonian](/wiki/Classical_mechanics "Classical mechanics")) analysis of [orbital mechanics](/wiki/Orbital_mechanics "Orbital mechanics") assumes that the more subtle effects of [general relativity](/wiki/General_relativity "General relativity"), such as [frame dragging](/wiki/Frame_dragging "Frame dragging") and [gravitational time dilation](/wiki/Gravitational_time_dilation "Gravitational time dilation") are negligible. Relativistic effects cease to be negligible when near massive bodies (as with the [precession of Mercury's orbit](/wiki/Kepler_problem_in_general_relativity "Kepler problem in general relativity") about the Sun),[[19]](#cite_note-O'Donnell_2014-19) or when extreme precision is needed (as with calculations of the [orbital elements](/wiki/Orbital_elements "Orbital elements") and time signal references for [GPS](/wiki/Global_Positioning_System#Relativity "Global Positioning System") satellites.[[56]](#cite_note-56))

Because of general relativity, there exists a smallest possible radius for which a particle can stably orbit a [black hole](/wiki/Black_hole "Black hole"). Any inward perturbation to this orbit will lead to the particle spiraling into the black hole. The size of this [innermost stable circular orbit](/wiki/Innermost_stable_circular_orbit "Innermost stable circular orbit") depends on the spin of the black hole and the [spin](/wiki/Spin_(physics) "Spin (physics)") of the particle itself,[[57]](#cite_note-57) but with no rotation the theoretical orbital radius is just three times the radius of the [event horizon](/wiki/Event_horizon "Event horizon").[[58]](#cite_note-58)

## Specification

Main article: [Ephemeris](/wiki/Ephemeris "Ephemeris")

See also: [Keplerian elements](/wiki/Keplerian_elements "Keplerian elements")

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/e/eb/Orbit1.svg/250px-Orbit1.svg.png)](/wiki/File:Orbit1.svg)

Elements of an elliptical orbit, showing the orbital plane alignment is given by the inclination, longitude of the ascending node and argument of periapsis

Six parameters are required to specify a [Keplerian orbit](/wiki/Keplerian_orbit "Keplerian orbit") about a body. For example, the three numbers that specify the body's initial position and the three values that specify its velocity will define a unique orbit that can be calculated forwards (or backwards) in time.[[59]](#cite_note-59)

An [unperturbed](/wiki/Perturbation_theory "Perturbation theory") orbit is two-dimensional in a plane fixed in space, known as the [orbital plane](/wiki/Orbital_plane "Orbital plane"). In three dimensions, the orientation of this plane relative to a [plane of reference](/wiki/Orbital_plane_of_reference "Orbital plane of reference"), such as the [plane of the sky](/wiki/Plane_of_the_sky "Plane of the sky"), can be determined by three angles. Extending the analysis to three dimensions requires simply rotating the two-dimensional plane to the required angles relative to the poles of the planetary body involved.

By tradition, the standard set of orbital elements is called the set of [Keplerian elements](/wiki/Orbital_elements "Orbital elements"), after Johannes Kepler and his laws. These six Keplerian elements are as follows:[[60]](#cite_note-60)

* [Inclination](/wiki/Inclination "Inclination") (*i*)
* [Longitude of the ascending node](/wiki/Longitude_of_the_ascending_node "Longitude of the ascending node") (Ω)
* [Argument of periapsis](/wiki/Argument_of_periapsis "Argument of periapsis") (ω)
* [Eccentricity](/wiki/Orbital_eccentricity "Orbital eccentricity") (*e*)
* [Semimajor axis](/wiki/Semimajor_axis "Semimajor axis") (*a*)
* [True anomaly](/wiki/True_anomaly "True anomaly") at [epoch](/wiki/Epoch_(astronomy) "Epoch (astronomy)") (*θ*0).

The [orbital period](/wiki/Orbital_period "Orbital period") is simply how long an orbiting body takes to complete one orbit, which can be derived from the semimajor axis, and the combined masses. In principle, once the orbital elements are known for a body, its position can be calculated forward, and backward indefinitely in time. However, in practice, orbits are affected or [perturbed](/wiki/Perturbation_(astronomy) "Perturbation (astronomy)"), by other forces than simple gravity from an assumed point source, and thus the orbital elements change over time.

Note that, unless the eccentricity is zero, *a* is not the average orbital radius. The time-averaged orbital distance is given by:[[61]](#cite_note-61)

:   ![{\displaystyle {\bar {r}}=a\left(1+{\frac {e^{2}}{2}}\right)}](https://wikimedia.org/api/rest_v1/media/math/render/svg/8eb2dff2276fddcdfdddc6b67d817867530637c6)

which only equals *a* when *e* is zero, for a circular orbit.

## Perturbations

Main article: [Perturbation (astronomy)](/wiki/Perturbation_(astronomy) "Perturbation (astronomy)")

Further information: [Osculating orbit § Perturbations](/wiki/Osculating_orbit#Perturbations "Osculating orbit"), and [Orbit modeling § Perturbations](/wiki/Orbit_modeling#Perturbations "Orbit modeling")

An orbital perturbation is when a force or impulse causes an acceleration that changes the parameters of the orbit over time. This perturbation is much smaller than the overall force or average impulse of the main gravitating body. Potential sources of perturbation include departure from sphericity, third body contributions, [radiation pressure](/wiki/Radiation_pressure "Radiation pressure"), [atmospheric drag](/wiki/Atmospheric_drag "Atmospheric drag"), and [tidal acceleration](/wiki/Tidal_acceleration "Tidal acceleration").[[62]](#cite_note-62)

### Radial, transverse and normal perturbations

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/d/df/Hohmann_transfer_orbit.svg/250px-Hohmann_transfer_orbit.svg.png)](/wiki/File:Hohmann_transfer_orbit.svg)

A [Hohmann transfer orbit](/wiki/Hohmann_transfer_orbit "Hohmann transfer orbit") is a [maneuver](/wiki/Orbital_maneuver "Orbital maneuver") to change the altitude of an orbit (from 1 to 3) with two tangential [impulses](/wiki/Impulse_(physics) "Impulse (physics)") (Δv and Δv')[[63]](#cite_note-63)

For a body in orbit, a perturbing force can be divided into three [orthogonal](/wiki/Orthogonality "Orthogonality") components: radial, transverse and normal. The first two are in the orbital plane (in the direction of the gravitating body and along the path of a circular orbit, respectively) and the third is away from the orbital plane.[[64]](#cite_note-64) A small radial impulse given to a body in orbit changes the [eccentricity](/wiki/Eccentricity_(mathematics) "Eccentricity (mathematics)"), but not the [orbital period](/wiki/Orbital_period "Orbital period") (to first order). A [prograde](/wiki/Direct_motion "Direct motion") or [retrograde](/wiki/Retrograde_motion "Retrograde motion") transverse impulse (i.e. an impulse applied along the orbital motion) changes both the eccentricity and the [orbital period](/wiki/Orbital_period "Orbital period"). Notably, a prograde impulse at periapsis raises the altitude at apoapsis and vice versa and a retrograde impulse does the opposite. A normal impulse (out of the orbital plane) causes rotation of the [orbital plane](/wiki/Orbital_plane_(astronomy) "Orbital plane (astronomy)") without changing the [period](/wiki/Orbit_(dynamics) "Orbit (dynamics)") or eccentricity. In all instances, a closed orbit will still intersect the perturbation point.

### Orbital decay

Main article: [Orbital decay](/wiki/Orbital_decay "Orbital decay")

For an object in a sufficiently close orbit about a planetary body with a significant atmosphere, the orbit can decay because of [drag](/wiki/Drag_(physics) "Drag (physics)").[[65]](#cite_note-Vukovich_Kim_2019-65) Particularly at each periapsis for an orbital with appreciable eccentricity, the object experiences atmospheric drag, losing energy. Each time, the orbit grows less eccentric (more circular) because the object loses kinetic energy precisely when that energy is at its maximum.[[66]](#cite_note-66) This is similar to the effect of slowing a pendulum at its lowest point; the highest point of the pendulum's swing becomes lower. Eventually, the effect becomes so great that the maximum kinetic energy is not enough to return the orbit above the limits of the atmospheric drag effect. When this happens the body will rapidly spiral down and intersect the central body.

The region for experiencing atmospheric drag varies by planet; a re-entry vehicle needs to draw much closer to Mars than to Earth,[[67]](#cite_note-67) for example, and the drag is negligible for Mercury. The bounds of an atmosphere vary significantly due to [solar forcing](/wiki/Solar_forcing "Solar forcing") and [space weather](/wiki/Space_weather "Space weather").[[68]](#cite_note-68) During a [solar maximum](/wiki/Solar_maximum "Solar maximum"), the Earth's atmosphere causes drag up to a hundred kilometres higher than during a solar minimum.

Orbits can be artificially influenced through the use of rocket engines, which change the kinetic energy of the body at some point in its path. In this way, changes in the orbit shape or orientation can be facilitated. [Solar sails](/wiki/Solar_sail "Solar sail") or [magnetic sails](/wiki/Magnetic_sail "Magnetic sail") are forms of propulsion that require no propellant or energy input other than that of the Sun, and so can be used indefinitely for station keeping.[[69]](#cite_note-69)[[70]](#cite_note-70) (See [statite](/wiki/Statite "Statite") for one such proposed use.) Satellites with long conductive tethers can experience orbital decay because of electromagnetic drag from the [Earth's magnetic field](/wiki/Earth%27s_magnetic_field "Earth's magnetic field").[[71]](#cite_note-71) As the wire cuts the magnetic field it acts as a generator, moving electrons from one end to the other. The orbital energy is converted to heat in the wire.

For objects below the [synchronous orbit](/wiki/Synchronous_orbit "Synchronous orbit") for the body they're orbiting, orbital decay can occur due to [tidal forces](/wiki/Tidal_force "Tidal force").[[72]](#cite_note-72) The gravity of the orbiting object raises [tidal bulges](/wiki/Tidal_bulge "Tidal bulge") in the primary, and since it is below the synchronous orbit, the orbiting object is moving faster than the body's surface so the bulges lag a short angle behind it. The gravity of the bulges is slightly off of the primary-satellite axis and thus has a component along the direction of the satellite's motion. The near bulge slows the object more than the far bulge speeds it up, and as a result, the orbit decays.

Conversely, the gravity of the satellite on the bulges applies [torque](/wiki/Torque "Torque") on the primary and speeds up its rotation. Artificial satellites are too small to have an appreciable tidal effect on the planets they orbit, but several moons in the Solar System are undergoing orbital decay by this mechanism.[[73]](#cite_note-73) Mars' innermost moon [Phobos](/wiki/Phobos_(moon) "Phobos (moon)") is a prime example and is expected to either impact Mars' surface or break up into a ring in 20 to 40 million years.[[74]](#cite_note-74)

Orbits can decay via the emission of [gravitational waves](/wiki/Gravitational_wave "Gravitational wave"). This mechanism is extremely weak for most stellar objects, only becoming significant in cases where there is a combination of extreme mass, and extreme acceleration, such as [compact objects](/wiki/Compact_object "Compact object") that are orbiting each other closely.[[75]](#cite_note-75)

### Oblateness

The standard analysis of orbiting bodies assumes that all bodies consist of uniform spheres, or more generally, concentric shells each of uniform density. Mathematically, such bodies are gravitationally equivalent to point sources per the [shell theorem](/wiki/Shell_theorem "Shell theorem").[[76]](#cite_note-76) However, in the real world, many bodies rotate and this introduces [oblateness](/wiki/Oblateness "Oblateness"); known as an [equatorial bulge](/wiki/Equatorial_bulge "Equatorial bulge"). This adds a [quadrupole moment](/wiki/Quadropole#Gravitational_quadrupole "Quadropole") to the gravitational field, which is significant at distances comparable to the radius of the body.[[77]](#cite_note-77)[[78]](#cite_note-78) In the general case, the gravitational potential of a rotating body such as a planet can be [expanded in multipoles](/wiki/Multipole_expansion "Multipole expansion") to account for the departure from spherical symmetry.[[79]](#cite_note-79)

From the point of view of satellite dynamics, of particular relevance are the so-called even zonal harmonic coefficients, or even zonals, since they induce secular orbital perturbations which are cumulative over time spans longer than the orbital period.[[80]](#cite_note-80)[[81]](#cite_note-81) They do depend on the orientation of the body's symmetry axis in the space, affecting, in general, the whole orbit, with the exception of the semimajor axis.

### Tidal locking

Main article: [Tidal locking](/wiki/Tidal_locking "Tidal locking")

Tidal locking between a pair of co-orbiting astronomical bodies occurs when one of the objects reaches a state where there is no longer any net transfer of [angular momentum](/wiki/Angular_momentum "Angular momentum") over the course of a complete orbit.[[82]](#cite_note-82) Their gravitational interaction forces steady changes to their orbits and rotation rates as a result of [energy exchange](/wiki/Energy_transfer "Energy transfer") and heat [dissipation](/wiki/Dissipation "Dissipation") until the locked state is formed. The object tends to stay in this state because leaving it would require adding energy back into the system. An example is the planet Mercury, which is locked in a state of completing three rotations about its axis for every two orbits.[[83]](#cite_note-83)

In the case where a tidally locked body possesses synchronous rotation, the object takes just as long to rotate around its own axis as it does to revolve around its partner. In this case, one side of the celestial body is permanently facing its host object. This is the case for the Earth's Moon and for both members of the Pluto-Charon system.[[84]](#cite_note-84)

### Multiple gravitating bodies

Main article: [n-body problem](/wiki/N-body_problem "N-body problem")

[![Apsidal precession refers to the rotation of the Moon's elliptical orbit over time, with the major axis completing one revolution every 8.85 years.](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/5/53/Moon_apsidal_precession.png/250px-Moon_apsidal_precession.png)](/wiki/File:Moon_apsidal_precession.png)

Apsidal precession of the Moon's elliptical orbit (not to scale and eccentricity exaggerated)

The effects of other gravitating bodies can be significant. For example, the [orbit of the Moon](/wiki/Orbit_of_the_Moon "Orbit of the Moon") cannot be accurately described without allowing for the action of the Sun's gravity as well as the Earth's.[[85]](#cite_note-85) One approximate result is that bodies will usually have reasonably stable orbits around a heavier planet or moon, in spite of these perturbations, provided they are orbiting well within the heavier body's [Hill sphere](/wiki/Hill_sphere "Hill sphere").[[86]](#cite_note-86)

A long-term impact of multi-body interactions can be [apsidal precession](/wiki/Apsidal_precession "Apsidal precession"), which is a gradual rotation of the [line between the apsides](/wiki/Apse_line "Apse line"). For an elliptical system, the result is a [rosetta orbit](/wiki/Rosetta_orbit "Rosetta orbit"). The ancient Greek astronomer [Hipparchus](/wiki/Hipparchus "Hipparchus") noted just such an apsidal precession of the Moon's orbit, as the revolution of the Moon's apogee with a period of approximately 8.85 years.[[87]](#cite_note-87) Apsidal precession can result from tidal perturbation, rotational perturbation, general relativity,[[19]](#cite_note-O'Donnell_2014-19) or a combination of these effects. The detection of apsidal precession in a distant [binary star system](/wiki/Binary_star_system "Binary star system") can be an indicator of the purturbative effect of an unseen third stellar companion.[[88]](#cite_note-88)

When there are more than two gravitating bodies it is referred to as an [n-body problem](/wiki/N-body_problem "N-body problem"). Most n-body problems have no [closed form solution](/wiki/Closed-form_expression "Closed-form expression"), although some special cases have been formulated.

### Approaches to many-body problems

Main article: [Orbit modeling](/wiki/Orbit_modeling "Orbit modeling")

Rather than an exact closed form solution, orbits with many bodies can be approximated with arbitrarily high accuracy. One method is to take the pure elliptic motion as a basis and add [perturbation](/wiki/Perturbation_(astronomy) "Perturbation (astronomy)") terms to account for the gravitational influence of multiple bodies.[[89]](#cite_note-89) This is convenient for calculating the positions of astronomical bodies. The equations of motion of the moons, planets and other bodies are known with great accuracy, and are used to generate [tables](/wiki/Ephemeris "Ephemeris") for [celestial navigation](/wiki/Celestial_navigation "Celestial navigation").[[90]](#cite_note-90) Still, there are [secular phenomena](/wiki/Secular_phenomena "Secular phenomena") that have to be dealt with by [post-Newtonian](/wiki/Parameterized_post-Newtonian_formalism "Parameterized post-Newtonian formalism") methods.

An incremental approach uses [differential equations](/wiki/Differential_equation "Differential equation") for scientific or mission-planning purposes.[[91]](#cite_note-91) According to Newton's laws, each of the gravitational forces acting on a body will depend on the separation from the sources. Therefore, accelerations can be expressed in terms of positions. The perturbation terms are much easier to describe in this form. Predicting subsequent positions and velocities from initial values of position and velocity corresponds to solving an [initial value problem](/wiki/Initial_value_problem "Initial value problem"). Numerical methods calculate the positions and velocities of the objects a short time in the future, then repeat the calculation ad nauseam. However, tiny arithmetic errors from the limited accuracy of a computer's math are cumulative, which limits the long-term accuracy of this approach.

Differential simulations with large numbers of objects perform the calculations in a hierarchical pairwise fashion between centers of mass. Using this scheme, galaxies, star clusters, and other large assemblages of objects have been simulated.[[92]](#cite_note-92)

### Radiation and magnetic fields

For smaller bodies particularly, light[[65]](#cite_note-Vukovich_Kim_2019-65) and [stellar wind](/wiki/Stellar_wind "Stellar wind") can cause significant perturbations to the [attitude](/wiki/Attitude_(geometry) "Attitude (geometry)") and direction of motion of the body, and over time can be significant. Objects with residual magnetic fields can interact with a planetary [magnetosphere](/wiki/Magnetosphere "Magnetosphere"), perturbing their orbit.[[65]](#cite_note-Vukovich_Kim_2019-65) Of the planetary bodies, the motion of [asteroids](/wiki/Asteroid "Asteroid") is particularly affected over large periods by the [Yarkovsky effect](/wiki/Yarkovsky_effect "Yarkovsky effect") when the asteroids are rotating relative to the Sun.[[93]](#cite_note-93)

## Strange orbits

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/4/4f/Klemperer_rosette.svg/250px-Klemperer_rosette.svg.png)](/wiki/File:Klemperer_rosette.svg)

A simple hexagonal [Klemperer rosette](/wiki/Klemperer_rosette "Klemperer rosette") with two kinds of body,[[94]](#cite_note-94) which [Klemperer](/wiki/Wolfgang_Klemperer "Wolfgang Klemperer") notes is the nearest to being stable.

Mathematicians have discovered that it is possible in principle to have multiple bodies in non-elliptical orbits that repeat periodically, although most such orbits are not stable regarding small perturbations in mass, position, or velocity. However, some special stable cases have been identified, including a planar figure-eight orbit occupied by [three moving bodies](/wiki/Three-body_problem "Three-body problem").[[95]](#cite_note-95) Further studies have discovered that nonplanar orbits are also possible, including one involving 12 masses moving in 4 roughly circular, interlocking orbits [topologically](/wiki/Topology "Topology") equivalent to the edges of a [cuboctahedron](/wiki/Cuboctahedron "Cuboctahedron").[[96]](#cite_note-Peterson-96)

Finding such orbits naturally occurring in the universe is thought to be extremely unlikely, because of the improbability of the required conditions occurring by chance.[[96]](#cite_note-Peterson-96)

## Astrodynamics

Main article: [Orbital mechanics](/wiki/Orbital_mechanics "Orbital mechanics")

Orbital mechanics or astrodynamics is the application of [ballistics](/wiki/Ballistics "Ballistics") and [celestial mechanics](/wiki/Celestial_mechanics "Celestial mechanics") to the practical problems concerning the motion of [rockets](/wiki/Rocket "Rocket") and other [spacecraft](/wiki/Spacecraft "Spacecraft").[[97]](#cite_note-97) The motion of these objects is usually calculated from [Newton's laws of motion](/wiki/Newton%27s_laws_of_motion "Newton's laws of motion") and [Newton's law of universal gravitation](/wiki/Newton%27s_law_of_universal_gravitation "Newton's law of universal gravitation"). It is a core discipline within space mission design and control. Celestial mechanics treats more broadly the orbital dynamics of systems under the influence of [gravity](/wiki/Gravity "Gravity"), including spacecraft and natural astronomical bodies such as star systems, [planets](/wiki/Planet "Planet"), [moons](/wiki/Natural_satellite "Natural satellite"), and [comets](/wiki/Comet "Comet"). Orbital mechanics focuses on spacecraft [trajectories](/wiki/Trajectory "Trajectory"), including [orbital maneuvers](/wiki/Orbital_maneuver "Orbital maneuver"), orbit plane changes and interplanetary transfers,[[98]](#cite_note-98) and is used by mission planners to predict the results of [propulsive maneuvers](/wiki/Spacecraft_propulsion "Spacecraft propulsion"). [General relativity](/wiki/General_relativity "General relativity") is a more exact theory than Newton's laws for calculating orbits, and is sometimes necessary for greater accuracy or in high-gravity situations (such as orbits close to the Sun or planets).[[99]](#cite_note-99)

## Earth orbits

Main article: [List of orbits](/wiki/List_of_orbits "List of orbits")

* [Low Earth orbit](/wiki/Low_Earth_orbit "Low Earth orbit") (LEO): [Geocentric orbits](/wiki/Geocentric_orbit "Geocentric orbit") with altitudes up to 2,000 [km](/wiki/Km "Km") (0–1,240 [miles](/wiki/Mile "Mile")).[[100]](#cite_note-100)
* [Medium Earth orbit](/wiki/Medium_Earth_orbit "Medium Earth orbit") (MEO): [Geocentric orbits](/wiki/Geocentric_orbit "Geocentric orbit") ranging in altitude from 2,000 [km](/wiki/Km "Km") (1,240 [miles](/wiki/Mile "Mile")) to just below [geosynchronous orbit](/wiki/Geosynchronous_orbit "Geosynchronous orbit") at 35,786 kilometers (22,236 mi). Also known as an [intermediate circular orbit](/wiki/Intermediate_circular_orbit "Intermediate circular orbit"). These are "most commonly at 20,200 kilometers (12,600 mi), or 20,650 kilometers (12,830 mi), with an orbital period of 12 hours."[[101]](#cite_note-nasa_orbit_definition-101)
* Both [geosynchronous orbit](/wiki/Geosynchronous_orbit "Geosynchronous orbit") (GSO) and [geostationary orbit](/wiki/Geostationary_orbit "Geostationary orbit") (GEO) are orbits around Earth matching Earth's [sidereal rotation](/wiki/Sidereal_rotation "Sidereal rotation") period. All geosynchronous and geostationary orbits have a [semi-major axis](/wiki/Semi-major_axis "Semi-major axis") of 42,164 km (26,199 mi). All geostationary orbits are also geosynchronous, but not all geosynchronous orbits are geostationary. A geostationary orbit stays exactly above the equator, whereas a geosynchronous orbit may swing north, and south to cover more of the Earth's surface. Both complete one full orbit of Earth per sidereal day (relative to the stars, not the Sun).[[102]](#cite_note-102)
* [High Earth orbit](/wiki/High_Earth_orbit "High Earth orbit"): [Geocentric orbits](/wiki/Geocentric_orbit "Geocentric orbit") above the altitude of [geosynchronous orbit](/wiki/Geosynchronous_orbit "Geosynchronous orbit") 35,786 [km](/wiki/Km "Km") (22,240 [miles](/wiki/Mile "Mile")).[[101]](#cite_note-nasa_orbit_definition-101)

## Scaling in gravity

|  |  |
| --- | --- |
|  | This section may contain information **not [important or relevant](/wiki/Wikipedia:What_Wikipedia_is_not#Encyclopedic_content "Wikipedia:What Wikipedia is not") to the article's subject.** Please help [improve this section](https://en.wikipedia.org/w/index.php?title=Orbit&action=edit). *(November 2025)* *([Learn how and when to remove this message](/wiki/Help:Maintenance_template_removal "Help:Maintenance template removal"))* |

The [gravitational constant](/wiki/Gravitational_constant "Gravitational constant") *G* has been calculated as:6.6743×10−11 m3⋅kg−1⋅s−2.[[103]](#cite_note-physconst-G-103)

* (6.6742 ± 0.001) × 10−11 (kg/m3)−1s−2.

Thus the constant has dimension density−1 time−2. This corresponds to the following properties.

[Scaling](/wiki/Scale_factor "Scale factor") of distances (including sizes of bodies, while keeping the densities the same) gives [similar](/wiki/Similarity_(geometry) "Similarity (geometry)") orbits without scaling the time: if for example distances are halved, masses are divided by 8, gravitational forces by 16 and gravitational accelerations by 2. Hence velocities are halved and orbital periods and other travel times related to gravity remain the same. For example, when an object is dropped from a tower, the time it takes to fall to the ground remains the same with a scale model of the tower on a scale model of the Earth.

Scaling of distances while keeping the masses the same (in the case of point masses, or by adjusting the densities) gives similar orbits; if distances are multiplied by 4, gravitational forces and accelerations are divided by 16, velocities are halved and orbital periods are multiplied by 8.

When all densities are multiplied by 4, orbits are the same; gravitational forces are multiplied by 16 and accelerations by 4, velocities are doubled and orbital periods are halved.

When all densities are multiplied by 4, and all sizes are halved, orbits are similar; masses are divided by 2, gravitational forces are the same, gravitational accelerations are doubled. Hence velocities are the same and orbital periods are halved.

In all these cases of scaling. if densities are multiplied by 4, times are halved; if velocities are doubled, forces are multiplied by 16.

These properties are illustrated in the formula (derived from the [formula for the orbital period](/wiki/Orbital_period#Small_body_orbiting_a_central_body "Orbital period"))

:   ![{\displaystyle GT^{2}\rho =3\pi \left({\frac {a}{r}}\right)^{3},}](https://wikimedia.org/api/rest_v1/media/math/render/svg/f6ec5a9836f4acf84cfe6b2aebffca080ac9813c)

for an elliptical orbit with [semi-major axis](/wiki/Semi-major_axis "Semi-major axis") *a*, of a small body around a spherical body with radius *r* and average density *ρ*, where *T* is the orbital period. See also [Kepler’s third law](/wiki/Kepler%E2%80%99s_third_law "Kepler’s third law").
